subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 10! In this unit you will become confident with angles – the amount of turn between two lines that meet at a point. Angles are everywhere once you start looking for them: in the corner of a page, in the hands of a clock, in the slope of a roof over a Hargeisa home, and in the turn a footballer makes before striking the ball toward the goal. In this unit you will learn to name angles, estimate their size, measure them accurately with a protractor, draw them yourself, and use clever angle rules to work out sizes you cannot see directly.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain what an angle is and name its parts: the vertex and the two arms.
Understand that angles are measured in degrees (°), and that a full turn is 360°.
Identify and classify angles as acute, right, obtuse, reflex or straight.
Estimate the size of an angle using reference angles such as 45°, 90° and 180°.
Measure an angle accurately with a protractor, choosing the correct scale.
Draw an angle of a given size with a protractor and ruler.
Use the rule that angles on a straight line add up to 180°.
Use the rule that angles around a point add up to 360°.
Use vertically opposite angles (equal angles where two lines cross).
Use the fact that the angles in a triangle add up to 180° to find a missing angle.

concepts.title: What Is an Angle?
concepts.explanation: An angle is the amount of turn between two straight lines that meet at a point. There are three helpful ways to picture the very same idea: as the opening between two lines, as the amount of rotation from one direction to another, and as the corner where two lines meet. All three describe one thing – how much turn there is. Every angle has three parts, and you should be able to name them. The two straight lines are called the arms (sometimes called rays). The point where the two arms meet is called the vertex. The…
concepts.example: Here is the single most important idea in the whole unit, so read it twice. The size of an angle depends only on the amount of turn, not on the length of the arms. Imagine two angles: one drawn with short arms only two…

concepts.title: Measuring Angles in Degrees
concepts.explanation: Angles are measured in degrees, written with the small raised symbol °. Degrees tell you how much turn an angle has. To understand the size of a degree, it helps to think about turning all the way around in a circle. Picture yourself standing and spinning slowly. If you turn all the way around and end up facing the same way you started, you have made a full turn, which is 360°. If you turn only halfway, so you are now facing the opposite direction, you have made a half turn, which is 180°. If you make a quarter…

concepts.title: Types of Angles
concepts.explanation: Mathematicians sort angles into five types by their size. Learning to name them on sight is an essential skill, so study each type carefully and use the memory helpers to keep them straight. 1. Acute angles are between 0° and 90°. They are the small, sharp, pointed angles – smaller than the corner of a book. The angle between clock hands at 1:00, the point of a slice of mango, and the sharp tip of a pencil are all acute. A memory helper: acute sounds like *a cute* little angle, small and sweet.
concepts.example: 2. Right angles are exactly 90°, like the corner of a square, a window, or a book. A right angle is the meeting of a wall and the floor. Right angles are so important that we mark them with a small square in the corner…

concepts.title: Estimating Angles
concepts.explanation: Before you ever pick up a protractor, you should estimate the size of an angle. Estimating first is a habit good mathematicians keep, because it warns you if your measured answer comes out badly wrong. The secret is to compare the angle you see with a few reference angles you know by heart. The most useful reference angle is the right angle, 90° – the corner of a book, a page, or a window. Ask yourself first: is this angle bigger or smaller than a right angle? If it is smaller, it is acute; if bigger, it is…
concepts.example: 90° – the corner of a book or sheet of paper (your most important reference).

concepts.title: Using a Protractor to Measure Angles
concepts.explanation: A protractor is a tool, usually a clear half-circle of plastic, marked with degrees from 0° to 180°. It is the instrument you use to measure an angle accurately instead of just estimating it. Learning to use a protractor carefully is a key skill of this unit, so follow these steps slowly the first few times. Place the small centre point (the little cross or hole in the middle of the flat edge) exactly on the vertex of the angle.
concepts.example: Turn the protractor so that one arm of the angle lies along the zero line (the line through 0°).

concepts.title: Drawing Angles with a Protractor
concepts.explanation: Once you can measure an angle, drawing one of a given size is only a small step further. Suppose you want to draw an angle of exactly 65°. Here is the careful method. Use a ruler to draw one straight arm, and mark a dot at one end to be the vertex.
concepts.example: Place the protractor's centre point on the vertex, with the arm along the zero line.

methods.title: How to measure an angle with a protractor
methods.example: Name the type of each angle: a) 35° b) 90° c) 145° d) 250°.
steps: Put the protractor's centre point exactly on the vertex.
Line up one arm along the zero line.
Check the how to measure an angle with a protractor result carefully.

methods.title: Estimate first: is the angle acute or obtuse?
methods.example: An arm crosses the protractor where both 40° and 140° are printed. The angle looks clearly smaller than a right angle. What is its size?
steps: Read where the other arm crosses, choosing the scale that matches your estimate.
Check the estimate first: is the angle acute or obtuse? result carefully.
Check the estimate first: is the angle acute or obtuse? result carefully.

methods.title: How to draw an angle of a given size
methods.example: Two angles sit on a straight line. One is 115°. Find the other.
steps: Draw one arm with a ruler and mark the vertex.
Place the protractor's centre on the vertex, arm on the zero line.
Make a mark at the size you want, counting from the correct zero.
Join the vertex to the mark with a ruler and label the angle.

methods.title: How to find a missing angle
methods.example: Four angles meet at a point: 90°, 100°, 80° and one unknown. Find the unknown angle.
steps: Decide the situation: straight line (180°), point (360°) or triangle (180°).
Add up all the angles you already know.
Subtract that total from 180° or 360° to find the missing angle.
Check your answer by adding all the angles back together.

methods.title: Vertically opposite angles
methods.example: Two straight lines cross. One of the four angles is 50°. Find the angle vertically opposite it, and one of the angles next to it.
steps: Solution: The vertically opposite angle is equal, so it is also 50°.
An angle next to the 50° sits on a straight line with it, so it is 180° − 50° = 130°.
The crossing has two angles of 50° and two of 130°.

methods.title: A missing angle in a triangle
methods.example: A triangle has angles of 70° and 55°. Find the third angle.
steps: Solution: The angles in a triangle add up to 180°.
Add the known ones: 70° + 55° = 125°.
So the third angle is 55°.

workedExamples.title: Name the type of angle
workedExamples.prompt: Name the type of each angle: a) 35° b) 90° c) 145° d) 250°.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: a) 35° is between 0° and 90°, so it is acute. b) 90° is exactly a right angle, so it is a right angle. c) 145° is between 90° and 180°, so it is obtuse. d) 250° is between 180° and 360°, so it is a reflex angle.

workedExamples.title: Choose the right protractor scale
workedExamples.prompt: An arm crosses the protractor where both 40° and 140° are printed. The angle looks clearly smaller than a right angle. What is its size?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: An angle smaller than a right angle is acute, so it must be less than 90°. Of the two readings, only 40° is acute. The answer is 40°. Estimating first is what tells us to pick 40° and not 140°.

workedExamples.title: Angles on a straight line
workedExamples.prompt: Two angles sit on a straight line. One is 115°. Find the other.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Angles on a straight line add up to 180°. So the other angle is 180° − 115° = 65°. Check: 115° + 65° = 180°. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Angles around a point
workedExamples.prompt: Four angles meet at a point: 90°, 100°, 80° and one unknown. Find the unknown angle.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Angles around a point add up to 360°. Add the known ones: 90° + 100° + 80° = 270°. Then 360° − 270° = 90°. Check: 90° + 100° + 80° + 90° = 360°. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Vertically opposite angles
workedExamples.prompt: Two straight lines cross. One of the four angles is 50°. Find the angle vertically opposite it, and one of the angles next to it.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The vertically opposite angle is equal, so it is also 50°. An angle next to the 50° sits on a straight line with it, so it is 180° − 50° = 130°. The crossing has two angles of 50° and two of 130°.

workedExamples.title: A missing angle in a triangle
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has angles of 70° and 55°. Find the third angle.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The angles in a triangle add up to 180°. Add the known ones: 70° + 55° = 125°. Then 180° − 125° = 55°. So the third angle is 55°.

workedExamples.title: Equal slices around a point
workedExamples.prompt: A round anjero is cut into 8 equal slices from the centre. What is the angle of each slice, and what type of angle is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The slices share a full turn of 360° equally, so each is 360° ÷ 8 = 45°. Since 45° is between 0° and 90°, each slice is an acute angle.

workedExamples.title: An angle of a regular pentagon
workedExamples.prompt: The five angles of a regular pentagon add up to 540°. Find the size of each angle.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: All five angles are equal, so share the total: 540° ÷ 5 = 108°. Each interior angle of a regular pentagon is 108°, which is obtuse.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has angles of 80° and 60°. Find the third angle.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A round cake is cut into 5 equal slices from the centre. What is the angle of each slice?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Estimate, then say the type: an angle that looks a little larger than a right angle is about ___° and is ___.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: The reflex angle on one side of two arms is 310°. What is the ordinary angle on the other side, and what type is it?

activities.title: Human Angle Maker
activities.materials: your body, and a friend to help.
steps: Use your two arms to make different angles: a right angle (arms like an L), an acute angle (arms close together), and an obtuse angle (arms wide apart).
Make a straight angle by stretching both arms out in a straight line.
Play the guessing game: a friend makes an angle with their arms and you name the type.
Challenge: can you make a reflex angle with your arms by measuring the long way around?
Making angles with your body helps you *feel* the difference between the types.

activities.title: Clock Angle Detective
activities.materials: a clock or a drawing of a clock face, and a protractor.
steps: Draw clock faces showing 3:00, 6:00, 9:00 and 12:00, and mark the angle between the hands.
Which of these times show a right angle? Which show a straight angle?
Draw 1:00 and 2:00. Are the angles between the hands acute or obtuse?
Challenge: what time makes the smallest angle between the hands? What time makes 90° again after 3:00?

activities.title: Angle Scavenger Hunt
activities.materials: a notebook or a camera, and a protractor if you have one.
steps: Hunt indoors for ten angles: corners of books and windows, open scissors, a leaning broom, a door ajar.
For each one, first estimate the size, then measure it if you can.
Sort your findings into acute, right, obtuse and reflex.
Outdoor hunt: find angles in tree branches, building corners and playground bars, and classify them too.

activities.title: Fold-a-Protractor and Angle Art
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, and coloured pencils.
steps: Fold a square of paper corner to corner to make a 45° fold, then fold again to make a smaller angle. What angle is it now?
Use a ruler to draw four small artworks: one using only right angles, one using only acute angles, one using only obtuse angles, and one mixing all three.
Label each artwork with the angle types you used.
Show a family member and ask them to find and name the angles in your art.

activities.title: Robot Turning Program (Investigation)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and either a toy robot or your own two feet.
steps: To draw a square, a robot repeats: move forward, turn 90°, four times. Walk it out and check you end up facing the way you started.
To draw a triangle, a robot turns 120° at each corner, three times. Try it.
Investigate: what turn angle would a robot need at each corner to draw a regular hexagon (6 turns)? A regular pentagon (5 turns)?
Hint: the turns must add up to one full turn of 360°. So the hexagon turn is 360° ÷ 6, and the pentagon turn is 360° ÷ 5.

activities.title: Angle Games Tournament (Family or Group)
activities.materials: paper cards and a protractor for checking.
steps: Angle Charades: act out an angle type with your arms and let others guess acute, right, obtuse or reflex.
Quick Draw: someone calls out an angle size, players race to draw it, then check with a protractor.
Angle Addition: given two angles on a straight line, be first to work out the missing angle to 180°.
Keep score across all three games. The winner is the family Angle Champion!

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity helped you understand angle types best? Where do you see angles most often in your daily life? What was the trickiest part of measuring with a protractor? How might a builder, a tailor or a footballer use angles? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Types of Angles Acute: greater than 0° and less than 90°. Right: exactly 90° (marked with a small square). Obtuse: greater than 90° and less than 180°. Straight: exactly 180° (a straight line). Reflex: greater than 180° and less than 360°.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Turn Facts A full turn is 360°. A half turn is 180°. A quarter turn is 90°.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Angle Rules Angles on a straight line add up to 180°. Angles around a point add up to 360°. Vertically opposite angles (where two lines cross) are equal. The three angles in any triangle add up to 180°.

terms: The amount of turn between two lines at a point
The unit for measuring an angle
The point where the two arms meet
One of the two lines forming the angle
A tool marked in degrees for measuring angles
Equal angles where two lines cross
commonMistakes: Compare the opening, ignore the arm length
Estimate first, then pick acute or obtuse reading
Put the centre exactly on the vertex
Measure the small angle, then 360° minus it

realProblems.prompt: Two straight roads cross in Nairobi. One of the four angles is 35°. Find the other three angles, and name the type of each.
realProblems.answer: 1) The opposite angle is 35° (acute); the other two are each 180° − 35° = 145° (obtuse).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) The opposite angle is 35° (acute); the other two are each 180° − 35° = 145° (obtuse).

realProblems.prompt: A door opens from 0° (closed) to 90° (fully open). It is currently open at 62°. How much further can it open, and what type of angle is the remaining opening?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Omar designs a logo with 5 equal rays coming out from a centre point. What angle is between each pair of rays?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A triangular garden beside the masjid has angles of 95° and 40°. Find the third angle, and say which types the three angles are.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Farah faces north and turns 180°. Which direction is she facing now, and what type of angle did she turn through?
realProblems.answer: South; she turned through a straight angle (180°).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: South; she turned through a straight angle (180°).

realProblems.prompt: A spotlight turns 90° to the right, then 135° more to the right. Through what total angle has it turned, and what type of angle is the total?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: The reflex angle on one side of two arms is 310°. What is the ordinary angle on the other side, and what type is it?
Three angles on a straight line are 40°, 65° and one unknown. Find the unknown.
A regular octagon has interior angles that add to 1080°. Find the size of each angle.
Two angles around a point are equal, and a third is 160°. What is the size of each of the two equal angles?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The two equal angles share 360° − 160° = 200°, so each is 200° ÷ 2 = 100°.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A triangle has one right angle and two equal remaining angles. Find the size of each of the two equal angles.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The two equal angles share 180° − 90° = 90°, so each is 45°.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Drawing Angles with a Protractor.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Acute / Right means Less than 90° / exactly 90°.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: 90°–180° / exactly 180°?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: Greater than 180° and less than 360°?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Reflex means Greater than 180° and less than 360°.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: Equal angles where two lines cross?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Vertically opposite means Equal angles where two lines cross.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: The amount of turn between two lines at a point?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Angle means The amount of turn between two lines at a point.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: The unit for measuring an angle?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Degree (°) means The unit for measuring an angle.

questions.question: Which term matches this meaning: The point where the two arms meet?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 10 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Vertex means The point where the two arms meet.

selfAssessment: I can explain what an angle is and name its parts: the vertex and the two arms.
I can understand that angles are measured in degrees (°), and that a full turn is 360°.
I can identify and classify angles as acute, right, obtuse, reflex or straight.
I can estimate the size of an angle using reference angles such as 45°, 90° and 180°.
I can measure an angle accurately with a protractor, choosing the correct scale.
I can draw an angle of a given size with a protractor and ruler.
I can use the rule that angles on a straight line add up to 180°.
I can use the rule that angles around a point add up to 360°.
